NVIDIA的云游戏服务GeForce Now今天正式在ChromeOS上进行测试版测试，它正在帮助游戏PC以比你预期更快的速度过时。只要有一个良好的互联网连接和一台Chromebook，你就可以开始玩了！访问play.geforcenow.com并登录到您的GeForce Now帐户。就这样。你不需要下载专用的应用程序，因为它可以在Chrome浏览器中正常工作。这有点像Stadia的工作原理，因为它们都使用WebRTC(网络实时通信)将游戏流传输到云端，但在PC上的GeForce Now和ChromeOS之间有一个关键的区别，这可能会影响你的游戏在较低带宽下的表现。
虽然云游戏在移动设备上一直举步维艰--在很大程度上它被排除在移动设备最大的平台之一之外，但它在PC和Mac上是完全可以访问的。但除了Stadia和少数第三方应用程序外，浏览器和Chromebook中一直没有云游戏。现在GeForce已经推出了一个基于Web的系统，它使GeForce在精细的网络控制方面比Stadia有了很大的优势。不幸的是，其中一些控件并不像我希望在GeForce now的基于网络的解决方案中看到的那样健壮。
一些重要的自定义设置现在都在ChromeOS上，比如最大比特率、分辨率和帧率，但它不仅缺少PC版本上的数据服务器和具有竞争力的流媒体质量预设，还缺少自动调整恶劣网络条件的能力，这会降低分辨率，以在低下载速度下实现可播放的帧率。
NVIDIA表示，用户将需要至少15 Mbps的互联网连接，以避免滞后和其他令人不快的视觉副作用-尽管25 Mbps或更高的连接将产生最好的效果，即使是在5.0 GHz的WiFi连接上也是如此。这对很多Chromebook用户来说是个好消息。但看起来，如果没有针对糟糕的网络条件自动调整的选项，游戏将无法在25 Mbps或更低的速度下玩。
当我最后一次在PC上以5 Mbps的连接速度测试GeForce Now时，尽管图像的像素化程度很高，但画面仍然很平滑。但当我将Pixelbook的带宽限制在25 Mbps，即NVIDIA推荐的下载速度时，游戏就无法玩了。它是像素化的，我移动得如此缓慢，如果不是因为游戏中仍有声音传出，我会认为游戏已经冻结了。当我将下载速度提高到35 Mbps时，游戏运行得近乎完美，除了这里和那里有一点滞后和像素化。
它真正闪耀的地方是输入延迟。当涉及到云游戏时，输入延迟比分辨率和帧率重要得多。输入延迟越低，您在屏幕上看到注册命令的速度就越快。测试“古墓丽影”在高速下载(285+Mbps)的情况下，Pixelbook上的GeForce现在通过5.0 GHz无线连接，性能几乎与我本地通过以太网连接的高规格PC相同，38ms到25ms。GeForce Now的延迟比我预期的要低得多。
当然，通过云玩游戏的输入延迟总是比本地游戏多，但这里的结果是如此接近，以至于几乎否定了PC游戏更好的论点，因为输入延迟更少。你的眼睛不会察觉到13毫秒的差异。
但GeForce现在的加载时间确实比本地PC更长，因为NVIDIA的服务器需要首先与Steam/Etic/Uplay的服务器连接，然后才能真正加载到游戏中。然而，当涉及到跨不同数字店面的加载时间时，那里的时间几乎相等。
我没有很多游戏的副本，但我可以比较一下蒸汽上的塔科马和史诗游戏商店上的塔科马的加载时间。使用秒表，我计算了从每个库加载游戏所需的时间。它花了40秒加载到蒸汽的主菜单中，而史诗花了44秒。这大约是从台式电脑加载的两倍，但有了稳定的互联网连接，通过云在Pixelbook上玩同一个游戏和在本地玩相同的游戏没有明显的区别，所以这在很大程度上弥补了这一点。
但是，有时游戏会完全拒绝加载，因为它实际上需要与您从其购买游戏的启动器不同的启动器。例子：我拥有史诗游戏商店的2区，但要玩那款游戏，我仍然必须推出Uplay。(PC也是如此。这是我最恼火的事，但那是另一天的另一次谈话。)。GeForce现在有点卡住了，当我第一次尝试加载第二分区时，它完全冻结了。我不得不退出游戏，并再次尝试让它工作，即使我能够加载它，也需要大约两到三分钟才能进入主菜单。我没有同样的问题加载远哭5从蒸汽，然而，因为该游戏也需要Uplay来运行。这可能是因为自从我先加载了分区2之后，GeForce现在已经将我的账号与Uplay关联起来了。
在“古墓丽影”中运行快速基准测试，在1080p和最高图形设置下，平均每秒84帧。在1080p的超音速下，第二分区是128fps，而在超音速1080p的情况下，远哭5是53fps。但是，虽然这些结果很好地证明了驱动NVIDIA云游戏服务器的硬件，但现在GeForce上的云游戏作为一个整体也有不利的一面-实际上是作为一个整体的云游戏。
流式视频被限制为30fps或60fps，因为这是GeForce现在仅有的两个选项，无论您的屏幕刷新率是否更高。在上面的游戏中，我的刷新率上限为60 Hz，我通过在基准测试时运行Fraps验证了这一点。(注：我必须在我的Windows PC上进行测试，因为ChromeOS上不能使用Fraps，但结果是一样的，因为两个屏幕的刷新率都是相同的。)。
Fps计数器根据NVIDIA服务器中的硬件显示渲染的帧率，但Fraps只显示60fps，因为它的读数基于GeForce Now启动器，后者会将镜头从服务器流式传输到Pixelbook或您正在使用的任何设备。当然，这有点令人沮丧，但你现在几乎可以在任何现代设备(iOS除外)上免费使用GeForce，60fps对任何玩Chromebook的人来说都很好。
如果您已经拥有一台Chromebook或正在考虑购买一台，NVIDIA已经在几种不同型号的Chromebook上测试了GeForce Now的兼容性，到目前为止已经在内部验证了以下几点，但不建议在列出的带星号的型号上使用语音聊天：
一旦NVIDIA增加了针对ChromeOS糟糕的网络条件进行自动调整的选项，游戏很可能会以25 Mbps或更低的速度运行。如果你的互联网很差或不可靠，ChromeOS目前不是使用GeForce的最佳平台，然而，NVIDIA表示，随着时间的推移，它将为ChromeOS增加更多的网络设置，对于那些拥有快速稳定的互联网连接的人，GeForce现在Pixelbook上以1080p的无延迟运行，几乎是本地PC输入延迟速度。
只要你不想玩暴雪娱乐(Blizzard Entertainment)、2K游戏(2K Games)、贝塞斯达(Bethesda)、华纳兄弟(Warner Bros)、Xbox游戏工作室(Xbox Game Studios)、Codemaster或Klei Entertainment的游戏-就像这些开发商/发行商现在从GeForce撤出他们的游戏一样-最好还是放弃买一台游戏笔记本电脑的想法。如果你需要一些便宜的东西让你的孩子只扮演堡垒之夜，你可能会考虑Chromebook和GeForce Now的组合。
note_en:
Nvidia’s cloud gaming service, GeForce Now,   officially hit beta on ChromeOS today and it’s helping to make the gaming PC obsolete faster than you’d expect. Just a good internet connection and a Chromebook, and off you go! Visit   play.geforcenow.com and log into your GeForce Now account. That’s it. You don’t need to download a dedicated app, as it works right in the Chrome browser. It’s sort of like how Stadia works in the sense that they both use WebRTC (Web Real-Time Communications) to stream your game over the cloud, but there is a key difference between GeForce Now on PC and ChromeOS, which could affect how your game performs at lower bandwidths.
    While cloud gaming has struggled on mobile—where it’s been   largely kept off one of mobile devices’ largest platforms, it’s totally accessible on the PC and Mac. But apart from Stadia and a handful of third-party apps, cloud gaming has been absent from the browser and Chromebooks. Now GeForce Now has rolled out a web-based system and it has given   GeForce Now a major edge over Stadia when it comes to granular network controls. Unfortunately, some of those controls aren’t as robust as I’d like to see on GeForce Now’s web-based solution.
 A few important, custom settings are on ChromeOS right now, like max bit rate, resolution, and frame rate, but not only is it missing the Data Server and Competitive streaming quality presets that are on the PC version, it’s also missing the ability to automatically adjust for poor network conditions, which lowers the resolution to achieve a playable frame rate at low download speeds.
  Nvidia says users will need a minimum 15 Mbps internet connection to avoid lag and other unpleasant visual side effects—although a 25 Mbps connection or greater will yield the best results, even on a 5.0 GHz wifi connection. That’s good news for a lot of Chromebook users. But it appears that without the option to automatically adjust for poor network conditions, games are unplayable at 25 Mbps and lower.
 When I   last tested GeForce Now on PC at a 5 Mbps connection, the frame was still smooth even though the graphics were heavily pixelated. But when I limited the bandwidth on the Pixelbook to 25 Mbps, Nvidia’s recommended download speed, the game was unplayable. It was pixelated, and I moved so sluggishly I would have thought the game was frozen had it not been for the fact that sound was still coming from the game. When I bumped up the download speed to 35 Mbps, the game ran near perfectly, save for a tiny bit of lag and pixelation here and there.
    One place it really shined was input latency. Input latency matters much, much more than resolution and frame rate when it comes to cloud gaming. The lower the input latency, the faster you’ll see your commands registered on screen. Testing  Shadow of the Tomb Raider at a high download speed (285+ Mbps), GeForce Now on the Pixelbook via a 5.0 GHz wireless connection performed nearly identical to my local, high-specced PC connected via Ethernet, 38ms to 25ms. The GeForce Now latency is much lower than I expected.
  Sure, there’s always going to be more input latency playing games via the cloud than locally, but the result is so close here that it  almost voids the argument that PC gaming is better because there’s less input latency. Your eyes aren’t going to detect a 13ms difference.
 But GeForce Now does have longer loading time than a local PC, because Nvidia’s servers need to connect with Steam/Epic/Uplay’s servers first before you can actually load into the game. However, when it comes to loading times across different digital storefronts, the time there is pretty much even.
  I don’t own duplicate copies of many games, but I was able to compare loading times between  Tacoma on Steam and  Tacoma on Epic Games Store. Using a stop watch, I timed how long it took to load the game from each library. It took 40 seconds to load into the main menu on Steam, verses 44 seconds for Epic. That is roughly twice as long compared to loading from a desktop PC, but with a solid internet connection, there’s no perceivable difference between playing the same game on a Pixelbook via the cloud and locally, so that makes up for it for the most part.
 However, sometimes a game will refuse to load altogether because it actually requires a different launcher than the one you bought the game from. Example: I own  The Division 2 on the Epic Games Store, but to play that game I still have to launch Uplay. (Same thing goes for PC. It’s a pet peeve of mine, but that’s another conversation for another day.) GeForce Now kind of choked the first time I tried loading  The Division 2. It totally froze. I had to exit the game and try again for it to work, and even though I was able to get it to load, it took about two to three minutes to get into the main menu. I didn’t have the same issue loading  Far Cry 5 from Steam, however, as that game also needs Uplay to run. That might be because since I loaded  The Division 2 first, GeForce Now already associated my account with Uplay.
  Running a quick benchmark test in  Shadow of the Tomb Raider, at 1080p and at the highest graphical setting, yielded an average of 84 frames per second.  The Division 2 was 128 fps at 1080p on ultra, and  Far Cry 5 was 53 fps at 1080p on ultra. But while these results are a good testament to the hardware powering Nvidia’s cloud gaming servers, there’s a downside to cloud gaming on GeForce Now as a whole—cloud gaming as a whole, actually.
   Streamed video is limited to 30 fps or 60 fps, as those are the only two options in GeForce Now, regardless of if your screen’s refresh rate is higher. In the games above, my refresh rate was capped at 60 Hz, and I verified this by running Fraps while benchmarking. (Note: I had to test this on my Windows PC because Fraps is not available for ChromeOS, but the result would be the same since both screens were capped at the same refresh rate.)
 The fps counter showed what frame rate was rendering based on the hardware in Nvidia’s server, but Fraps only showed 60 fps because it was basing its reading off the GeForce Now launcher, which is streaming the footage from the server to the Pixelbook or whatever device you’re using. Sure, it’s kind of a bummer, but you can use GeForce Now on nearly any modern device (except for iOS) for free, and 60 fps is just fine for anyone playing on a Chromebook.
  If you already own a Chromebook or are now thinking of getting one, Nvidia has tested GeForce Now on several different models of Chromebooks for compatibility, and so far has internally verified the following, but does not recommend using voice chat on the models listed with an asterisk:
  Once Nvidia adds the option to automatically adjust for poor network conditions for ChromeOS, games will most likely become playable at 25 Mbps and under. If you have poor or unreliable internet, ChromeOS is not the best platform to use GeForce Now at the moment, however, Nvidia said that it will be adding more network settings over time for ChromeOS and for those with a fast and stable internet connection, GeForce Now runs lag-free at 1080p on the Pixelbook with near-local PC input latency speeds.
  As long as you’re not looking to play games from Blizzard Entertainment, 2K Games, Bethesda, Warner Bros, Xbox Game Studios, Codemasters or Klei Entertainment—as those developers/publishers pulled their games from GeForce Now—it might be a better deal to toss out the idea of getting a gaming laptop. If you need something cheap to get your kid who only plays  Fortnite, you might want to consider the Chromebook/GeForce Now combo.
title: NVIDIA的云游戏服务GeForce现已在Chromebook上推出测试版，但缺乏针对糟糕的网络条件进行自动调整的能力
title_en: Nvidia's cloud gaming service GeForce Now is now available on Chromebooks in beta, but lacks the ability to automatically adjust for poor network conditions

note:
把自己想成一个独立的实体可以减少焦虑，同时也会给你的信心和决心带来一些重大的好处。
世界顶级明星如何鼓起站在舞台上的姿态和决心，尽管有一场糟糕的表演带来的紧张和焦虑？对于碧昂斯和阿黛尔来说，秘诀在于创造了另一个自我。
碧昂斯的角色是自信而充满力量的“萨莎·威猛”，这让她的表演变得格外自信和性感。“通常当我听到和弦的时候，当我穿上细高跟鞋的时候，就像刚才你紧张的时候，…。然后萨沙猛烈地出现了，我的姿势和说话的方式以及一切都不同了，“她在2008年告诉奥普拉·温弗瑞(Oprah Winfrey)。这是一个她一直在使用的策略，直到2010年，她觉得自己已经足够成熟，可以避免心理上的拐杖了。
受到与碧昂斯的一次动情会面的启发，阿黛尔紧随其后，在2011年向“滚石”杂志讲述了她创作的“萨莎·卡特”。这个角色是碧昂斯饰演的萨莎凶猛的角色和(真正的)乡村音乐明星琼·卡特的结合。阿黛尔说，这个策略帮助她在突破性的一年里把每一场表现都发挥到了极致。
尽管虚构人物形象的体现看起来像是流行歌星的噱头，但新的研究表明，这一策略可能会带来一些真正的心理好处。采用另一个自我是“自我疏远”的一种极端形式，它包括从我们的直接感觉中后退一步，让我们能够更冷静地看待一个情况。
纽约州汉密尔顿学院心理学助理教授雷切尔·怀特说：“自我疏远给了我们一点额外的空间，让我们理性地思考这种情况。”它可以让我们控制焦虑等不良情绪，增强我们对挑战性任务的毅力，增强我们的自制力。
密歇根大学心理学教授Ethan Kross在过去十年中领导了这项研究的大部分工作，他的研究表明，即使是视角上的微小转变也能帮助人们控制自己的情绪。
在一项研究中，参与者被要求以两种不同的方式之一来思考未来的具有挑战性的事件，比如一次重要的考试。处于“沉浸”状态的那组人被告知要从内部描绘它，就好像他们处于情况的中间，而那些处于“远处”状态的人则被要求从远处描绘它--就像他们是墙上的一只苍蝇一样。差异是惊人的，与沉浸在其中的那组人相比，那些采取较远视角的人对事件的焦虑要少得多。这种自我疏远也鼓励了更大的自我效能感-一种他们可以主动应对情况并实现目标的感觉。
在其他实验中，参与者被要求做一个简短的公开演讲。事前，他们被建议使用第三人称(“David Feel…”)仔细考虑他们对挑战的情绪。就好像他们是一个独立的实体，而不是更身临其境的第一人称(“我感觉”)。就像远距离的可视化一样，这个建议是为了鼓励人们从外部的角度来看待情况。
心理距离的创造再次帮助参与者控制了他们的焦虑，降低了他们对情绪的主观评分和客观指标，比如通常伴随威胁事件的心率和血压的变化。根据要求对他们的表现进行评级的独立观察员的判断，这些更有信心的感觉反映在演讲本身的质量上。
自我疏远似乎使人们能够通过引导他们关注更大的图景来获得这些积极的影响-有可能将事件视为更广泛计划的一部分，而不是陷入眼前的感觉。这让一些研究人员怀疑，通过确保我们即使在分心的情况下也能专注于我们的目标，它是否也能提高自控力的要素，比如决心。
沿着这些思路，一项实验询问，如果人们在测试前被要求练习自我疏远，他们是否能够更好地专注于难解的字谜。在这种情况下，他们被要求用第二人称给自己提建议--“你要集中精力回答每个问题”--就好像他们是在和朋友说话，而不是对自己说话。除了提高整体表现外，这些影响还可以从测量他们对任务的态度的问卷中看到，这表明他们有更强烈的改善表现的意图。
通过增强他们的自我控制感，自我疏远还可以改善人们的健康行为。例如，它可以增加人们锻炼的意图，并帮助他们抵抗垃圾食品的诱惑。这绝非易事。明尼苏达大学社会心理学研究员塞琳娜·福尔曼说：“到目前为止，很少有自我控制策略能成功改善进食效果。”
福尔曼最近与克罗斯一起工作，要求参与者练习自我疏远，因为他们面临着各种食物的选择-例如，与糖果相比，水果。当参与者练习自我疏远时(例如，问“大卫想要什么？”，而不是“我想要什么？”)。他们更有可能选择更健康的选择。
虽然还需要进一步的研究来测试这种方法的长期益处，但福尔曼认为它可以被纳入各种不同的减肥计划中。“异地自言自语的简便性确实提供了令人兴奋的潜在应用，”她说。例如，它可以被整合到移动应用程序中，通过通知提醒你在计划用餐时以第三人称的方式思考。
自我疏远可以提高意志力的可能性对儿童心理学家来说尤其令人兴奋，因为自律被认为对学习成绩的影响与智商一样重要。
几年前，心理学助理教授怀特让一组六岁的孩子在电脑上进行了一项注意力测试，测试中一系列图像闪现，每当他们看到奶酪的图片时都必须按空格键。这项任务原本设计得相当枯燥，但孩子们被告知这是一项“非常重要的活动”，如果他们在这项任务上工作的时间尽可能长，他们就会是一个“好帮手”--这增加了他们坚持不懈的动力。作为一个潜在的分心因素，研究人员还给他们留了一台iPad，还有一款更有趣的游戏，旨在吸引他们离开。
事前，孩子们被告知，如果任务变得太无聊，有时想想他们的感受可能会有帮助。一些人被告知要思考“我在努力工作吗？”而其他人则被鼓励用第三人称思考(“汉娜在努力工作吗？”)。第三组可以选择通过扮演他们最喜欢的虚构英雄，如蝙蝠侠或探险家朵拉来完全改变角色。他们甚至得到了道具来打扮，当他们感到无聊时，他们被告知要把自己的行为当作真正的角色来考虑，比如，问他们，“蝙蝠侠在努力工作吗？”
研究人员曾怀疑，另一个自我会是一种更极端的自我疏远形式，结果恰恰表明了这一点。虽然以第三人称思考的孩子在这项任务上花费的时间比以第一人称思考的孩子多10%，但坚持时间最长的是居住在另一个自我中的孩子。总体而言，他们在这项任务上花费的时间比那些用第三人称思考的人多13%(比那些用第一人称思考自己的行为的人多23%)。
怀特还发现，采用另一个自我也可以帮助孩子们专注于复杂的纸牌游戏，在这场游戏中，他们必须遵循不断变化的复杂规则。再一次，“蝙蝠侠效应”似乎提高了他们的决心和注意力，改善了他们的“执行功能”。
虽然这些都是实验室实验，但怀特希望这个小练习可以缓解许多需要自我控制的情况。毕竟，对毅力的测试已经非常接近孩子们在做作业时可能面临的那种决定，他们可能会受到电视或手机的潜在诱惑。她认为，在新的挑战中避免感到沮丧也可能是有用的。“假装成一个更有能力的人，并远离这种情况，可以帮助他们克服在学习新东西时的挫折感。”
考虑到这些发现和更广泛的自我疏远的好处，怀特怀疑，我们都可以通过选择体现另一个萨沙·威猛的角色来提高我们的情绪调节、自我控制和总体镇定。
她说，毕竟，某些宗教倡议已经鼓励了这种思维。“当我在90年代长大的时候，所有这些手镯上都写着‘WWJD’--耶稣会怎么做？”她说。“所以，我认为对很多人来说，这是一个相当直观的概念。”
如果你想自己试一试，怀特建议为不同类型的目标选择不同的人-也许是解决个人困境的明智的家庭成员，或者是解决专业问题的工作导师。“当我还是一名博士后时，我们实验室里有一个小小的说法，如果你是本科生，就假装成研究生。如果你是研究生，就假装是博士后；如果你是博士后，就假装是实验室的领导--只是为了让你达到下一个层次，“她说。
无论我们选择什么样的角色，练习都应该创造一些心理空间，远离潜在的分心感觉，同时也提醒我们想要效仿的行为。无论我们是设身处地设身处地为朋友、宗教人物或碧昂斯本人着想，一点点想象力都会让我们更接近我们想要成为的人。
大卫·罗布森(David Robson)是“智力陷阱：为什么聪明人做蠢事”(The Intelligence Trap：为什么Smart People Do umb Things，WW Norton/Hodder&Amp；Stoughton)一书的作者，该书研究了非理性思维的前沿心理学，以及做出更明智决定的最佳方式。
note_en:
Thinking of yourself as a separate entity can reduce anxiety, while also kicking up some major benefits for your confidence and determination.
 How do the world’s top stars muster the poise and determination to stand on stage, despite the nerves and anxiety of having a bad performance? For both Beyoncé and Adele, the secret has been the creation of an alter ego.
 Beyoncé’s was the assertive and empowered ‘Sasha Fierce’, who allowed her to perform with extra self-confidence and sensuality. “Usually when I hear the chords, when I put on my stilettos, like the moment right before when you’re nervous… then Sasha Fierce appears, and my posture and the way I speak and everything is different,”  she told Oprah Winfrey in 2008. It was a strategy that she continued to use until 2010, when she  felt she had matured enough to avoid the psychological crutch.
 Inspired by an emotional meeting with Beyoncé herself, Adele followed suit, telling  Rolling Stone magazine in 2011 about her creation of ‘Sasha Carter’. The persona was a combination of Beyoncé’s Sasha Fierce persona and the (real) country music star June Carter. Adele said the strategy helped her give her best to every performance during her breakout year.
 Although the embodiment of a fictional persona may seem like a gimmick for pop stars, new research suggests there may be some real psychological benefits to the strategy. Adopting an alter ego is an extreme form of ‘self-distancing’, which involves taking a step back from our immediate feelings to allow us to view a situation more dispassionately.
 “Self-distancing gives us a little bit of extra space to think rationally about the situation,” says Rachel White, assistant professor of psychology at Hamilton College in New York State. It allows us to rein in undesirable feelings like anxiety, increases our perseverance on challenging tasks, and boosts our self-control.
  Ethan Kross, professor of psychology at the University of Michigan, has led much of this research over the past decade, showing that  even small shifts in perspective can help people to gain control of their emotions.
 In one study, participants were asked to  think about a challenging event in the future, such as an important exam, in one of two different ways. The group in the “immersed” condition were told to picture it from the inside, as if they were in the middle of the situation, whereas those in the “distanced” condition were asked to picture it from afar – as if they were a fly on the wall. The differences were striking, with those taking the distanced viewpoint feeling much less anxious about the event, compared to the immersed group. The self-distancing also encouraged greater feelings of self-efficacy – the sense that they could pro-actively cope with the situation and achieve their goal.
  In other experiments, participants were asked to give a small public talk. Beforehand, they were advised to think through their emotions about the challenge using the third person (“David feels…”) as if they were a separate entity, rather than the more immersive first person (“I feel”). Like the distanced visualisation, this advice was designed to encourage the person to see the situation from an outside perspective.
 Once again, the creation of the psychological distance helped the  participants to master their anxiety, reducing both their subjective ratings of the emotion and objective measures, such as the changes in heart rate and blood pressure that usually accompany threatening events. And  those feelings of greater confidence were reflected in the quality of the presentation itself, according to the judgement of independent observers asked to rate their performance.
  Self-distancing seems to enable people to reap these positive effects by leading them to focus on the bigger picture – it’s possible to see events as part of a broader plan rather than getting bogged down in immediate feelings. And this has led some researchers to wonder whether it could also improve elements of self-control like determination, by making sure that we keep focused on our goals even in the face of distraction.
  Along these lines, one experiment asked whether people were  better able to focus on difficult word puzzles if they had been asked to practice self-distancing before the test. In this case, they were asked to give advice to themselves in the second-person – “ You will concentrate on each question” – as if they were talking to a friend, rather than themselves. Besides improving overall performance, the effects could also be seen in questionnaires measuring their attitudes to the task, which revealed a stronger intention to improve their performance.
 By boosting their sense of self control, self-distancing can also improve people’s health behaviour. It can increase people’s intentions to exercise, for instance, and helps them to resist the temptation of junk food. This is no mean feat. “Few self-control strategies to date have successfully improved eating outcomes,” says Celina Furman, a social psychology researcher at the University of Minnesota.
  Working with Kross, Furman recently asked participants to practice self-distancing as they were faced with the choice of various kinds of food – fruit compared to candy, for example. When participants had practiced self-distancing (asking “What does  David want?”, for example, rather than “What do  I want?)  they were more likely to choose the healthier option.
 Although further studies will be needed to test the long-term benefits of the approach, Furman thinks that it could be incorporated into a variety of different weight-loss schemes. “The ease of distanced self-talk does offer exciting potential applications,” she says. It could be incorporated into mobile apps, for instance, with notifications that prompt you to think in the third person as you plan your meals.
  The possibility that self-distancing could increase willpower is particularly exciting for child psychologists, given that self-discipline is thought to be as important for academic performance as IQ.
 A few years ago, assistant psychology professor White took a group of six-year-olds and set them a test of concentration on a computer, in which a series of images flashed and they had to press the space bar whenever they saw a picture of cheese. The task was designed to be rather boring, but the children were told that it was “a very important activity” and that they would be a “good helper” if they worked on the task for as long as possible – increasing their motivation to persevere. As a potential distraction, the researchers also left them with an iPad, with a much more fun game designed to lure them away.
 Beforehand, the children were told that it could sometimes be helpful to think about their feelings, if the task got too boring. Some were told to think “Am  I working hard?” while others were encouraged to think in the third-person (“Is  Hannah working hard?”). A third group were given the option to change persona entirely by inhabiting the role of their favourite fictional hero, such as Batman or Dora the Explorer. They were even given props to dress up, and when they got bored, they were told to consider their behaviour as if they were the actual character, asking, for instance, “Is  Batman working hard?”
  The researchers had suspected that the alter ego would be a more extreme form of self-distancing, and the results showed exactly that. While the children thinking in the third person spent about 10% more time on the task that those thinking in the first person, it was the children inhabiting their alter egos who stuck it out for the longest of all. Overall,  they spent 13%more time on the task than those thinking in the third person (and 23% more than those thinking about their behaviour in the first person).
 White has also found that adopting an alter ego can also help children to concentrate on a complex card game, in which they had to follow complex rules that kept on changing. Once again, “ the Batman effect” seemed to have increased their resolve and concentration, improving their “executive function”.
 While these were laboratory experiments, White hopes that this small exercise might ease many situations that require self-control. The test of perseverance, after all, was already very close to the kind of decisions that children might face when doing their homework with the potential temptation of the TV or cell phone. She thinks it may also be useful to avoid feeling dispirited during new challenges. “Pretending to be someone who's more competent, and getting that distance from the situation, could help them to overcome the frustration they're feeling when they're learning something new.”
  Given these findings and the benefits of self-distancing more generally, White suspects that we could all boost our emotional regulation, self-control and general poise by choosing to embody another persona à la Sasha Fierce.
 After all, certain religious initiatives already encourage this kind of thinking, she says. “When I was growing up in the ‘90s, there were all these bracelets that said, ‘WWJD’ – What would Jesus do?” she says. “So, I think it’s a pretty intuitive concept to a lot of people.”
 If you want to try it yourself, White suggests picking a different person for different types of goals – maybe a wise member of your family for a personal dilemma, or a work mentor for a professional problem. “When I was a postdoc, we had a little saying in our lab that if you're an undergrad, pretend to be a grad student. If you are a grad student, pretend to be a postdoc, and if you're a postdoc, pretend to be the leader of the lab – just to get you to that next level,” she says.
 Whatever persona we choose, the practice should create some psychological space away from potentially distracting feelings, while also reminding us of the behaviours we want to emulate. Whether we’re placing ourselves in the shoes of a friend, a religious figure, or Beyoncé herself, a little imagination might put us all a little closer to the person we wish to become.
 David Robson is the author of   The Intelligence Trap: Why Smart People Do Dumb Things (WW Norton/Hodder & Stoughton) , which examines the cutting-edge psychology of irrational thinking and the best ways to make wiser decisions.
title: “蝙蝠侠效应”：拥有另一个自我如何赋予你力量
title_en: The ‘Batman Effect’: How having an alter ego empowers you

note: 2020年8月一些政客提议在所得税之外引入财产税。让我们试着对不同水平的财富税的影响进行建模，看看它们在实践中对初创公司创始人意味着什么。假设你在20多岁时成功创业，然后再活60年。你的财富税会消耗多少股票？假设财富税是1%。这意味着每年你可以保留99%的库存。这意味着60年后，你剩下的库存比例将是0.99^60，或者说.547。因此，1%的财富税意味着政府将在你的一生中拿走你45%的股票。这是政府在不同级别的财产税下将在60年内持有多少股票：乍一看，如此明显的低税率会产生如此戏剧性的影响，这可能会让人感到惊讶。2%的财富税意味着，如果你在20多岁时创办了一家成功的公司，政府将在你一生中拿走你三分之二的股票。超过5%，你就所剩无几了，结果就不值得计算了。财富税之所以有如此戏剧性的影响，是因为它们一遍又一遍地重新适用于同一笔钱。所得税每年都会征收，但只针对那一年的收入。然而，如果你在获得一些资产后活了60年，财产税将对同一资产征收60倍的税。一种财产税复合税。即使是征收0.5%的财产税，创始人也会开始远离征收财产税的州或国家。那比你库存的四分之一还多。
note_en: August 2020  Some politicians are proposing to introduce wealth taxes in additionto income taxes. Let's try modeling the effects of various levelsof wealth tax to see what they would mean in practice for a startupfounder.  Suppose you start a successful startup in your twenties, and thenlive for another 60 years. How much of your stock will a wealth taxconsume?  Suppose the wealth tax is 1%. That means each year you get to keep99% of your stock. Which means after 60 years the proportion ofstock you'll have left will be .99^60, or .547. So a 1% wealth taxmeans the government will over the course of your life take 45% ofyour stock.  Here's how much of your stock the government will take over 60years at various levels of wealth tax:     It may at first seem surprising that such apparently small tax ratesproduce such dramatic effects. A 2% wealth tax means that if youstart a successful company in your 20s, the government will over thecourse of your life take two thirds of your stock. And beyond 5% youhave so little left that the results aren't worth calculating.  The reason wealth taxes have such dramatic effects is that they'reapplied over and over to the same money. Income taxhappens every year, but only to that year's income. Whereas if youlive for 60 years after acquiring some asset, a wealth tax will taxthat same asset 60 times. A wealth tax compounds.  Even a .5% wealth tax would start to keep founders awayfrom a state or country that imposed it. That's more than a quarterof your stock.
title: 建立财产税模型
